title: A New Technique for Visualization of the Human Retinal Vasculature
abstract:
A New Technique for Visualization of the
Human Retinal Vasculature
Gerard A. Lutty, PhD, D. Scott McLeod
\s=b\Adenosine diphosphatase, an endo-
thelial cell ectoenzyme that hydrolyses
adenosine 5\m='\-diphosphateto adenosine
monophosphate, has been used histo-
chemically to study the human retinal
vasculature in flat mounts
or as flat-
embedded tissue that can be sectioned.
In all retinas, reaction product was con-
fined almost exclusively to the vascula-
ture, with arteries having more reaction
product than veins or capillaries. Adeno-
sine diphosphatase
activity
was
ob-
served in endothelium, smooth-muscle
cells, pericytes, and erythrocytes. In the
more elaborate version of the technique,
the adenosine diphosphatase\p=m-\incubated
retinas
were flat embedded
in glycol
methacrylate. This allowed study of the
retinal vasculature in dual perspective:
en bloc with the use of dark-field micros-
copy and specific areas of interest histo-
logically sectioned for further evaluation.
The technique is simple and preserves
angioarchitecture
because
the
retinas
are intact, and patency of the vasculature
can be determined.
(Arch Ophthalmol. 1992;110:267-276)
 "1 he angioarchitecture of the adult
human retina consists of a superfi¬
cial primary vasculature within the
nerve fiber layer and an anastomosing
deeper secondary capillary network
within the inner nuclear layer.1 The
secondary
network
ends
before
it
reaches the ora serrata, while a third
layer, the radial peripapillary capillar¬
ies, exists near the optic nerve head.
Because of the thin, transparent na¬
ture of retina and the distributional
arrangement of blood vessels within it,
the retinal vasculature lends itself to
flat-mount histologie preparations.
One of the earliest retinal flat-mount
techniques employed was perfusion of
the blood vessels with india ink, which
is still widely used in laboratory ani¬
mals.1,2 While visualization of patent
vessels can be achieved, the technique
is difficult to apply to the postmortem
human retina, because perfusion of any
tracer substance requires cannulation
and exsanguination. Trypsin digestion
of extravascular tissue with
subse¬
quent staining of the blood vessels was
introduced by Kuwabara and Cogan.3
With this technique, which has become
the standard for use in humans, even
nonpatent vessels can be seen if their
basement
membrane
remains.
One
technique that avoided tracers and di¬
gestion
of
nonvascular
components
was the alkaline phosphatase enzyme
histochemical
technique.
Nilhausen"
applied the technique to fetal human
retinas and visualized the established
vasculature and its precursors. Unfor¬
tunately, in adult tissue this enzyme is
expressed only within the arterial side
of the circulation5 (D.S. McLeod,
un¬
published data, 1985). Each of these
flat-mount techniques has contributed
greatly to our knowledge of the normal
human retinal vasculature and how it
is
affected by
disease.
While
flat
mounting does provide information re-
garding specific
sites
of pathology
within the retinal vasculature, section¬
ing must be performed to examine the
structural properties of blood vessels
and their association with the
sur¬
rounding tissue. Ideally, a preparation
that offers the combined advantages of
both techniques would be more useful
than either method alone.
With this goal in mind, we applied
another
enzyme
histochemical
tech¬
nique, magnesium-activated adenosine
triphosphatase (ATPase), to visualize
the developing neonatal canine retinal
vasculature. Initially, isolated retinas
were incubated for the histochemical
demonstration of ATPase activity and
then flat mounted.6 Both the endotheli¬
al cells of established vessels and pre¬
cursors, angioblasts,
were visualized
with this technique. The method was
refined further by flat embedding the
ATPase-reacted
retinas
in
glycol
methacrylate, which permitted visual¬
ization of the vasculature en bloc and
established sites where serial sections
could then
be accurately obtained.'
This provided a dual perspective of the
same blood vessels. A major shortcom¬
ing of the technique was that in dogs
older than 21 days of age, nonvascular
retinal structures become positive for
ATPase activity.
The discovery by Lessel and Kuwa¬
bara8
that
adenosine diphosphatase
(ADPase) activity was more specific
for the adult human vasculature than
was ATPase activity prompted us to
modify our technique for use in hu¬
mans.
Substitution of adenosine
di¬
phosphate (ADP) for adenosine tri-
phosphate (ATP) as substrate in the
Accepted for publication September 4, 1991.
From The Wilmer Ophthalmological Institute,
The Johns Hopkins School of Medicine, Baltimore,
Md (Dr Lutty), and The Johns Hopkins Universi-
ty Applied Physics Laboratory, Laurel, Md (Mr
McLeod).
Reprint requests to 170 Woods Research Build-
ing, The Johns Hopkins Hospital, 600 N Wolfe St,
Baltimore, MD 21205 (Dr Lutty).

diabetes mellitus; and CPA, cardiopulmonary arrest.
incubation solution permitted the tech¬
nique to be applied to human postmor¬
tem retinas. Herein,
we present the
application of the ADPase histochemi¬
cal technique to the study of human
retinal
vasculature
and
discuss the
benefits of this new approach.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Eyes
The identity of the donors of the cadaver
eyes used in this study remained anony¬
mous. The cause of death, age, postmortem
time, and brief medical history of each case
are presented in Tables 1 and 2.
ADPase Histochemical Study
Flat Mounting.—The anterior chamber
was removed by an incision made 1.0 cm
posterior to the limbus. The central vitre¬
ous was removed, and the retina was teased
from the eye wall with a cyclodialysis spatu¬
la. Retinas for flat mounting
were pro¬
cessed by a modification of the technique of
Flower et al.6 The retina was fixed over¬
night at 4°C in 10% neutral buffered formal¬
in (3.7% formaldehyde, vol/vol), washed in
cold tap water,
and then incubated for
ADPase activity at 37°C for 15 minutes. The
reaction solution contained 0.2 mol/L of
TRIS maléate buffer (pH 7.2), 3 mmol/L of
lead nitrate, 6 mmol/L of magnesium chlo¬
ride, and 1 mg of ADP per milliliter. The
retina was then rinsed five times in distilled
water, and the reaction product was devel¬
oped with 2% ammonium sulfide for 1 min¬
ute. After washing in distilled water, the
retina was flat mounted in glycerogel.
Flat Embedding. —Retinas
were
pro¬
cessed for flat embedding by a modification
of the technique of McLeod et al.' The
retinas were removed
as described above
and manipulated in 0.1 mol/L of cacodylate
buffer, pH 7.2, with 5%
sucrose at 4°C.
After dissection, the retinas were rinsed in
a series of 0.1-mol/L cacodylate buffers with
increasing concentrations of sucrose (5%,
10%, and 15%) for 15 minutes each at 4°C.
Retinas were fixed overnight in 2% parafor-
maldehyde in 0.1 mol/L of cacodylate buffer
at 4°C, washed in 0.1 mol/L of cacodylate
buffer with 15% sucrose for 30 minutes, and
then washed in 0.1 mol/L of cacodylate
buffer with 10%
sucrose for 30 minutes.
Incubation for ADPase activity
was per-
formed
as described above,
followed by
several washes in 0.1 mol/L of cacodylate
buffer with 5% sucrose. Retinas were float¬
ed
onto glass slides,
and several radial
incisions were made to permit flattening.
The vasculatures of these wet retinas were
examined microscopically under dark-field
illumination. Areas designated for section¬
ing were excised, refloated onto modified
Swinney (Gelman Sci, Ann Arbor, Mich)
filter holders, and overlaid with nylon mesh
secured with a retaining ring. This device,
which maintains the tissue in
a flattened
state, was used to carry the retinal pieces
through processing steps prior to embed¬
ding. Tissue was postfixed in 25%-strength
Karnovsky's
fixative
overnight
at
4°C,
washed in 0.1 mol/L of cacodylate buffer
with 5% sucrose, and dehydrated in graded
alcohols (30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 90%, 95%,
and 95%) for 10 minutes at each step.
Subsequently, the retinas were infiltrat¬
ed in catalyzed solution A (JB-4 kit, Polys-
ciences, Warrington, Pa) for 1.5 hours under
vacuum at
room temperature. Following
infiltration, retinas were transferred from
the "holders" to disc block embedding molds
containing freshly catalyzed solution A, to
which solution  was added (1 mL of solu¬
tion  per 25 mL of catalyzed solution A).
Polymerization occurred within 2 hours un¬
der
vacuum
at
room temperature.
The
blocks
were then stored in
a desiccated
environment until fully cured.
The cured blocks were placed on glass
slides with the vasculature nearest the ob¬
jective, and then immersion oil and a cover-
slip were applied. With dark-field illumina¬
tion, the lead phosphate reaction product in
the retinal vascular elements was revealed;
retinal vessels appeared bright against
a
clear, darker background. Micrographs of
the retinal vasculature were obtained under
dark-field illumination and
were also
re¬
corded electronically for subsequent image
analysis. Portions of the block were desig¬
nated for sectioning and rephotographed
after trimming. Trimmed regions designat¬
ed for en face sectioning were photographi¬
cally mapped to ensure documentation of
the
entire region
and
then
bonded
to
BEEM capsule (Polysciences) blanks. Two-
micron sections were cut with a dry glass
knife on a Sorvall ultramicrotome (Sorvall,
Norwalk, Conn) and dried on glass slides.
The longitudinally sectioned blocks
were
rephotographed after each sectioning ses¬
sion to document precise orientation of sec¬
tions from the tissue. We have used the
following histologie stains on JB-4 sections:
thionin; hematoxylin-eosin; colloidal iron for
acid mucopolysaccharides; periodic acid-S¬
chiff (PAS) for basement membranes, gly-
cogen, mucin, and reticulum; and Wright's
Giemsa stain for leukocyte identification.
The deposition of ADPase reaction product
was sometimes developed by exposing sec¬
tions before staining to ammonium sulfide
(Light Solution diluted 1:10 and incubated
for 1 minute; Fisher Scientific, Pittsburg,
Pa).
Image Analysis
Flat-mounted and flat-embedded ADPase
retinas were visualized under a dissecting

(Light Solution diluted 1:10 and incubated
for 1 minute; Fisher Scientific, Pittsburg,
Flat-mounted and flat-embedded ADPase
retinas were visualized under a dissecting
microscope (Wild Heerbrugg, Heerbrugg,
Switzerland) or a photomicroscope (Zeiss,
Madein, West Germany) with the use of
transmitted illumination for flat-mounted
retinas and darkfield illumination for flat-
embedded retinas. A video signal was ob¬
tained from a black-and-white video camera
(WD-CD50, Panasonic, Secaucus, NJ). Im¬
ages were digitized with a frame grabber
board (Data Translation, Marlburo, Mass)
and then imported into IMAGE software
(written by Wayne Rasband, National In¬
stitutes of Health, Bethesda, Md, version
1.35) for intensity profiling. The intensity
levels between 0 (white) and 255 (black).
Once the area of interest was chosen, a scan
of the density of the reaction product was
made. The information from the
presented as a histogram of reaction prod¬
uct density. The digitized images, whether
made binary by the IMAGE software pro¬
gram for area fraction or percent vascular
area determinations ([number of black pix¬
els/total number of pixels]  100).
Energy-Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS)
Energy-dispersive spectroscopy is a tech¬
nique used to identify compositional ele¬
ments by detecting characteristic X-rays
generated after a sample has been bom¬
barded with electrons. An EDS light ele¬
Princeton, NJ) was used for X-ray detec¬
tion, and the International Scientific Instru¬
Pleasantown, Calif, was used for the elec¬
through uranium can be specifically identi¬
fied with this system; therefore, it is useful
for the detection of calcium and phospho¬
rous in calcine emboli, for example. After
photography by polarized microscopy, the
unstained JB-4 sections were made conduc¬
tive by overcoating with approximately 20
nm of palladium that does not overlap with
the emissions of calcium and phosphorus.
Polarized light images were used to locate
emboli, and the EDS spectra were collect¬
ed. These spectra provided confirmation of
the composition of emboli detected histolog-
ically in serial sections. Energy-dispersive
spectroscopy might also be useful to identi¬
Human cadaver eyes from eight do¬
nors (Table 1) were processed as AD¬
Pase flat mounts. For this purpose, the
reaction product was developed with
lead sulfide reaction product visible
with bright-field microscopy wherever
product was almost exclusively in the
vasculature (Fig 1) (exceptions to this
rule will be discussed below). Overall,
in normal eyes, the most reaction prod¬
uct was in arteries, less was in veins,
and the least was in capillaries (Figs 1,
Fig 1.—Bright-field micrographs of adenosine diphosphatase (ADPase) retinal flat mounts. A,
Midperipheral retina of case 3 demonstrates the normal appearance of an artery, arterioles, and
capillaries (original magnification,  25).  , Higher magnification of the retina from case 3 in an
area with a side arm arteriolar branch. The arteriole (a) has greater ADPase activity than the
venule ( ) (original magnification  68). C, High magnification of a capillary in case 3. The
vessel's wall is ADPase positive, but the pericyte nucleus (p) lacks activity (original magnifica¬
tion  1000). D, Area of retina from case 8 that is comparable in vascular hierarchy with the
normal area shown in Fig 1, B. An arteriole with reduced ADPase activity branches and forms an
arteriovenous anastomosis (arrow) that has abnormally high enzyme activity (original magnifi¬
cation  68). E, Midperipheral retina from case 7 demonstrates areas of nonperfusion. The
artery (a) has branches that give rise to a limited capillary bed while the vein (v) has very few
branches (original magnification  25). F, Area of retina from case 4 that is comparable with the
field shown in Fig 1, B. A tortuous neovascular loop (arrow), which originates from a capillary,
has abnormally high ADPase activity (original magnification  68). G, Central area of the retina
from case 7 in which an artery (a) lacks a capillary-free zone. Multiple aneurysms are apparent in
adjacent capillaries (original magnification  68).  , Edge of the vasculature in case 2, a 29-
weeks' gestation premature human infant. The dilated peripheral vessels end in a mesenchymal
shunt, with avascular retina peripheral to the shunt (original magnification  68).
Fir  .—Terminal capillary bed in similar regions from four cases. In all micrographs, the artery is
ai die top and the vein is at the bottom. A, Bright-field micrograph of a retinal flat mount from case
3 shows artery, vein, and connecting capillary bed. B, Binary image of retinal field shown in Fig 2,
A. Thirty-two percent of the pixels are black; therefore, this field has a 32% vascular area. C,
Bright-field micrograph of an area comparable with Fig 2, A from a donor with insulin-dependent
diabetes mellitus (case 4). There is capillary dropout and probably poor perfusion in the area
between the artery and the vein. D, Binary image of the same field of retina shown in Fig 2, C. In
this area with compromised vasculature, the percent vascular area was 16%. E, Dark-field
micrograph of flat-embedded retina from case 13, a donor with non-insulin-dependent diabetes
mellitus. The en bloc appearance is reversed, but the vasculature is comparable with the flat
mount in Fig 2, A. F, Binary image of field shown in Fig 2, E. The percent vascular area was 31 %
for this field. G, Dark-field micrograph of a flat-embedded retina from case 16, a donor with
insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus with diabetic retinopathy. This area is comparable in
vascular hierarchy with the retinal field from a donor with non-insulin-dependent diabetes
mellitus shown in Fig 2, E. H, Binary image of area shown in Fig 2, G. In this area with vascular
dropout, the percent vascular area was 15% (all parts, original magnification  70).
A and  ). When the density of the
reaction product was determined by
image analysis in a normal retina (case
3), arteries had 25% more reaction
variable levels of reaction product de¬
pendent on anatomic location, ie, there
was more reaction product near the
with the highest levels of activity were
moses (Fig 1, D) and areas of neovas¬
cularization (Fig 1, F). Vessels
pected to have compromised blood flow
expressed little ADPase activity (Fig
1, E). Microaneurysms had variable
activity (Fig 1, G). The technique was
applied to developing retinal vascula¬
ture as well. The retina shown in Fig
1,  was from a 28-weeks'-gestation
premature human infant (birth weight,
660 g) who survived with supplemental
oxygen for 2.5 months before death.
The area of retina shown has a mesen¬
chymal shunt of the type often ob¬
served in retinas with retinopathy of
prematurity. This case demonstrated
also that ADPase was active in retinas
with postmortem times as long as 42
The ADPase flat-mount technique
was simple and useful for the examina¬
tion of the vasculature of eyes with
which autolysis may have occurred, ie,
where the quality of tissue preserva¬
tion might not yield interpretable his¬
tologie sections. Image analysis of vas¬
cular patterns was easily accomplished
with the flat mounts. For example,
when images from comparable areas of
vasculature in flat-mount retinas (Figs
2, A and C) were digitized and made
binary by the IMAGE software pro¬

the ability to section the retina in any
plane for the most advantageous analy¬
sis. Figure 7 is a montage of dark-field

microaneurysms. This block was sec¬

tissue adjacent to vessels and to better
describe the types of aneurysms and
their relationship to feeder vessels.

In the more elaborate form of the
technique—retinal tissue flat embed¬
in glycol methacrylate—retinal
vessels were viewed in dual perspec¬
tive: en bloc and in histologie section.
This was possible because polymerized
bright-field visualization and localiza¬
tion of pigmented anomalies en bloc,
eg, pigment epithelial migration, hem¬
salmon patches and black sunburst in
sickle cell disease, aspects not docu¬
mented herein. The benefits of glycol
methacrylate for the histopathologic
study of retina have been demonstrat¬
ed previously. McLeod et al7 utilized
this polymer to visualize retinal vascu¬
lature development in the dog. Nal-
bandian et al9 noted that this polymer
yielded artifact-free ocular sections.
We have attempted to further mini¬
mize artifacts by gradually elevating
the osmolality of the buffers before
fixation. An important advantage to
the flat-embedding technique is the
ab'lity to choose the place and plane in
which to section the retina, based on
the angioarchitecture observed in the
block. Tangential sections, although
not often used with light microscopy,
permitted observation of vaso-occlu-
sions and the proximal and distal ef¬
structure and contents, in addition to
visualization of the ischemie effects of
occlusion on adjacent sensory retina.
One of the few shortcomings of the
flat-embedding technique was uneven
distribution of ADPase activity at ma¬
head, where the retinal vasculature is
trilayered. This might have been due
to substrate availability in areas with
density of vessels, such as the optic
disc, where the amount of enzyme may
exceed the concentration of substrate
available. Even in these areas, howev¬
visualized en bloc. The only other ob¬
stacle to the demonstration of ADPase
activity is vitreous adherence to the
dissected retina. Vitreous appears to
nonspecifically bind lead and thus can
deprive the reaction of its phosphate
As we have demonstrated, the flat-
embedding technique was ideally suit¬
ed for studies of vaso-occlusive pro-
cesses. By combining the battery of
classic special stains afforded by glycol
methacrylate with the techniques of
polarization microscopy and EDS, one
can identify with excellent morpholo¬
gic detail all of the major contributors
to vaso-occlusion: cholesterol and cal¬
cific emboli, platelet/fibrin thrombi,
and sickled red blood cells. The method
expedited detailed histologie analysis,
because areas of compromised vascula¬
ture were first identified en bloc.
Image analysis was easily applied to
either flat-mounted or flat-embedded
retinas because the preparations had
high contrast. Vascular patterns and
changes in ADPase activity were ana¬
lyzed in both preparations. Computer-
assisted analysis of embedded retinas
was also useful to document areas of
The ADPase technique not only al¬
lows visualization of the vasculature
but also gives insights into ADP me¬
tabolism. Borgers et al10 observed at

activity in coronary vessels was pre¬
sent in the plasma membrane and pino-
firmed biochemically in vitro by the
studies of Cooper et al. " Wilson et al12

though intracellular activity was also

This may be extremely important in
diabetes, in which maximum aggrega¬
tion of platelets in response to ADP
occurs during insulin dependence.20
In diabetic retinopathy, the rate and
degree of ADP-stimulated aggregation
increase markedly. This creates a vi¬
cious cycle, because platelet aggrega¬
tion causes a further release of ADP
event. Khosla et al21 concluded from
their work that retinopathy and al¬
tered platelet function may be patho-
In conclusion, the ADPase flat-em¬
bedding technique allows analysis of
retinal vasculature patterns and struc¬
ture without exclusion of the abluminal
sensory retina. Therefore, application
of this method yields morphologic fi¬
delity of retinal angioarchitecture and
of its relationships to the neural milieu.
